questions.question: What are the three dimensions of food security?
questions.explanation: Availability means food production and stocks are sufficient; Accessibility means food is within reach; Affordability means individuals have money to buy nutritious food[cite: 1604, 1605, 1606].

questions.question: How did Amartya Sen redefine food security?
questions.correct_answer: By adding the dimension of 'access' through 'entitlements'.
questions.explanation: He emphasized that food security is a combination of what one can produce, exchange in the market, and state-provided supplies[cite: 1612].

questions.question: What was the most devastating famine in India, and how many died?
questions.correct_answer: The Famine of Bengal in 1943; it killed thirty lakh (3 million) people[cite: 1625].
questions.explanation: Agricultural labourers, fishermen, and casual labourers were affected the most by the rising price of rice[cite: 1627, 1628].

questions.question: What is the difference between 'Chronic Hunger' and 'Seasonal Hunger'?
questions.correct_answer: Chronic hunger is due to persistently inadequate diets; Seasonal hunger is related to cycles of food growing/harvesting[cite: 1709, 1713].
questions.explanation: Chronic hunger is a result of very low income, while seasonal hunger occurs when people are unable to find work for the entire year[cite: 1712, 1715].

questions.question: What are the two main components of India's food security system?
questions.correct_answer: (a) Buffer Stock and (b) Public Distribution System (PDS)[cite: 1789].
questions.explanation: The government uses these to ensure food availability and stabilize prices[cite: 1788, 1858].

questions.question: What is 'Minimum Support Price' (MSP)?
questions.correct_answer: A pre-announced price paid by the government to farmers for their crops[cite: 1794].
questions.explanation: It is declared before the sowing season to provide incentives to farmers for raising production[cite: 1795].

questions.question: What are the three types of ration cards in India?
questions.correct_answer: Antyodaya (poorest of the poor), BPL (below poverty line), and APL (above poverty line)[cite: 1809, 1810].
questions.explanation: These cards determine the amount and price of subsidized goods a family can purchase[cite: 1808].
questions.source_section: What is the Public Distribution System?

questions.question: Which act categorizes 75% of rural and 50% of urban populations as eligible for food security?
questions.explanation: This act provides for food and nutritional security at affordable prices[cite: 1837].
questions.source_section: Current Status of Public Distribution System

questions.question: What is the role of cooperatives in food security?
questions.correct_answer: Setting up shops to sell low-priced goods to the poor[cite: 1935].
questions.explanation: Examples include Mother Dairy in Delhi and Amul in Gujarat, which brought about the White Revolution[cite: 1937, 1938, 1939].
questions.source_section: Role of cooperatives in food security

questions.question: Why are overflowing FCI godowns considered a problem?
questions.correct_answer: It leads to high carrying costs, wastage, and deterioration of grain quality[cite: 1908].
questions.explanation: Massive stocks are undesirable when grains rot or are eaten by rats instead of reaching the needy[cite: 1862, 1907].
questions.source_section: Current Status of Public Distribution System

notes.title: Dimensions of Food Security
notes.content: Availability (production/stocks), Accessibility (within reach), and Affordability (financial capacity to buy)[cite: 1604, 1605, 1606].

notes.title: Famine vs. Starvation
notes.content: Famine is characterized by widespread deaths due to starvation and epidemics caused by contaminated water and loss of body resistance[cite: 1622, 1624].

notes.title: Buffer Stock Mechanics
notes.content: FCI procures wheat and rice at MSP and distributes them at an 'Issue Price' lower than market rates in deficit areas[cite: 1791, 1797].

facts.fact: India became self-sufficient in foodgrains in the last 30 years due to the Green Revolution[cite: 1737].
Total foodgrain production in India reached 315 million tonnes in 2021-22[cite: 1730].
There are approximately 5.5 lakh ration shops (Fair Price Shops) across India[cite: 1805, 1806].
facts.source_section: What is the Public Distribution System?

facts.fact: In Tamil Nadu, around 94% of fair price shops are run by cooperatives[cite: 1936].
facts.source_section: Role of cooperatives in food security
